questions.question: Explain the purpose of the TTL field in IPv4 header. How does it prevent infinite loops?
keyPoints: TTL is an 8-bit field that limits the lifetime of a datagram
It is decremented by 1 at each router hop
When TTL reaches 0, the datagram is discarded
An ICMP Time Exceeded message may be sent to source
This prevents datagrams from circulating endlessly in case of routing loops

questions.question: What is IP fragmentation? Describe the conditions under which fragmentation occurs.
keyPoints: Fragmentation is splitting a large IP datagram into smaller pieces
It occurs when datagram size exceeds outgoing interface MTU
Each fragment has its own IP header with modified fields
Fragmentation happens at routers (not source or destination)
Reassembly only at destination to avoid overhead at intermediate routers

questions.question: Explain the role of the Identification, Flags, and Fragment Offset fields in IPv4 fragmentation.
keyPoints: Identification: 16-bit field uniquely identifies all fragments of same datagram
Flags: Reserved (always 0), DF (Don't Fragment), MF (More Fragments)
Fragment Offset: Position of fragment data in original datagram (in 8-byte blocks)
All fragments of same datagram share same Identification value
Last fragment has MF=0, others have MF=1

questions.question: Describe the IPv4 header checksum calculation and its purpose.
keyPoints: Header checksum is 16-bit one's complement of one's complement sum
Calculated over all 16-bit words in the header
Recalculated at each router because TTL changes
Provides error detection for header only, not data
If checksum fails, packet is discarded

questions.question: What happens when a router receives a packet larger than its MTU with DF flag set to 1?
keyPoints: DF=1 means Don't Fragment is enabled
Router cannot fragment the packet
Packet is discarded at the router
Router sends ICMP Destination Unreachable (Type 3, Code 4) to source
Source may need to fragment or find another path

questions.question: List and explain the fixed fields in the IPv4 header (without options).
keyPoints: Version (4 bits): IP version, always 4
IHL (4 bits): Header length in 32-bit words
TOS (8 bits): Type of Service for QoS
Total Length (16 bits): Total datagram size in bytes
Source/Destination Address (32 bits each): End-to-end addresses
TTL, Protocol, Checksum as discussed

questions.question: Explain why IPv4 fragmentation is performed only at routers and not at the source host.
keyPoints: Source may not know the entire path MTU
Path MTU can vary between different links
Each router knows its outgoing interface MTU
Doing it at routers handles varying MTUs dynamically
Reassembly only at destination simplifies intermediate routers

questions.question: With the help of a diagram, explain the IPv4 datagram header format. Describe the function of each field in detail.
keyPoints: Draw header showing 20-byte minimum structure
Version (4 bits): Protocol version number
IHL (4 bits): Header length in 32-bit words, min 5
TOS (8 bits): Type of Service/Precedence
Total Length (16 bits): Total datagram size
Identification (16 bits): Unique ID for fragmentation
Flags (3 bits): Reserved, DF, MF
Fragment Offset (13 bits): Position in 8-byte units
TTL (8 bits): Hop limit
Protocol (8 bits): Upper layer protocol
Header Checksum (16 bits): Error detection
Source Address (32 bits): Sender IP
Destination Address (32 bits): Receiver IP
Options (variable): Optional control info

questions.question: A host needs to send a 4000-byte IP datagram over a network with MTU of 1500 bytes. Explain the fragmentation process showing how each field is modified in the fragments.
keyPoints: Original: 4000 bytes, IP header 20 bytes, data 3980 bytes
MTU 1500 means max data per fragment = 1500-20 = 1480 bytes
Fragment 1: Data 1480 bytes, Offset 0, MF=1, ID same
Fragment 2: Data 1480 bytes, Offset 185 (1480/8), MF=1, ID same
Fragment 3: Data 1020 bytes (3980-2960), Offset 370 (2960/8), MF=0, ID same
Each fragment gets new header with modified Total Length, MF, Offset
Identification same for all fragments
Explain why offset is multiple of 8

questions.question: Explain the process of IP fragmentation and reassembly. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of fragmentation.
keyPoints: Fragmentation: Router splits datagram when size > MTU
Each fragment becomes independent packet with modified header
Reassembly: Only at destination host, not intermediate routers
Disadvantages: Overhead (bandwidth, processing), loss of one fragment loses entire datagram, security implications
Advantages: Allows communication across networks with different MTUs, enables end-to-end communication without source knowing all MTUs
Path MTU Discovery as modern alternative
